title: DTCC project in focus: Urban Trees
abstract: Trees are essential for healthy and resilient cities. But helping them thrive in dense urban areas requires more than just planting them. Through simulations and practical tools, the DTCC project Urban Trees works to make trees a natural part of long-term urban infrastructure.
summary: Trees are essential for healthy and resilient cities. But helping them thrive in dense urban areas requires more than just planting them. Through simulations and practical tools, the DTCC project Urban Trees works to make trees a natural part of long-term urban infrastructure.
intro: Trees are essential for healthy and resilient cities. But helping them thrive in dense urban areas requires more than just planting them. Through simulations and practical tools, the DTCC project Urban Trees works to make trees a natural part of long-term urban infrastructure.
body:
Trees play a vital role in shaping liveable urban environments. They cool overheated streets, influence wind and noise conditions, support biodiversity, and improve human wellbeing. In the DTCC-project Urban Trees, research lead Franziska Hunger and colleagues are developing simulations and data-driven methods to support smarter planning and long-term maintenance of urban trees.

“Urban trees provide many important ecosystem services but integrating them optimally into the urban environment is complex. Different goals can interact or even contradict each other, so we need better tools and methods to understand those trade-offs”, says Franziska Hunger.

One example concerns the maintenance of trees. Older trees provide greater ecosystem services and are more important for biodiversity, but they also carry increased risks, particularly during strong winds. The project studies how to translate results from digital tools, which could be used by arborists to make more well-informed decisions when maintaining old trees.

“Arborists understand the biology of trees very well. But they usually don’t have access to detailed information about the surrounding environment, such as wind conditions. If a tree stands in a sheltered courtyard where wind speeds rarely reach extreme levels, then using a generic storm wind value may overestimate the actual risk. With pre-calculated wind maps, arborists could instead base their assessment on location-specific wind conditions.”

From simulations to practical tools

The Urban Trees project builds on the DTCC milestone project Urban Environmental Comfort Design, which ran from 2020-2025, also led by Franziska Hunger. Working with both municipality and industry partners, the focus has shifted to vegetation.

The project is closely linked to the EU-funded Urban ElemenTree collaboration and involves partners from several European countries. An important part of the work is exploring how existing municipal data can be translated into action.

“The municipalities we work with are collecting a lot of data, but it’s not always clear how to use it effectively. We want to show what is useful, which workflows and tools exist, and how it can support real planning and maintenance decisions.

As part of the project, the research team, in close collaboration with the Urban ElemenTREE project, recently organised an international stakeholder workshop bringing together municipalities and consultancies from Sweden, Poland, Belgium, and Denmark. The three-hour session allowed practitioners to present real challenges from their daily work while researchers shared insights from existing tools and workflows.

“It was important that it wasn’t just us presenting. We also wanted to understand their workflows, their challenges, and the decisions they are facing. A key takeaway was the extent of data fragmentation within municipalities, where valuable datasets often exist but remain unknown or inaccessible across departments. It became clear that improving data flows is just as crucial as developing new analytical tools.”

Supporting long-term infrastructure

Looking ahead, the Urban Trees project aims to support cities in treating trees as long-term infrastructure rather than short-term additions. By strengthening the link between research, digitalization, and everyday practice, the initiative contributes to more resilient and climate-adaptive urban environments.

“We are not just talking about planting more trees. We are working toward a smarter way of planning and maintaining them so they can deliver benefits for generations to come”, says Hunger.
